sections.text: The singer identifies himself and the ballad's hero (lines 1-5). Rukiza's father died before the hero's birth; he grew up in the Baganga clan (lines 6-8).

As he grew to manhood, he became concerned with the state of the cattle his father bequeathed to him; investigating, he found their pasture was not adequate (lines 9-11.1). Rukiza urges his mother to migrate with him to seek better pastures, but his mother first requires that he take a wife before emigrating and then refuses to go; he leaves her cattle and herders and emigrates (lines 12-21).
The names of the clan members who accompany him are mentioned and foreshadow themes in the action (lines 22-34.1)

Rukiza searches for a place to settle with adequate and safe pastures, at first not finding any (lines 35-41). In Kabaale in Ihangiro Kingdom he finds his goal and inquires about the local ruler (lines 41.1-47).
Sending cattle as tribute to the king, Rukiza requests a place to settle (lines 48-53). The king grants his request and Rukiza has his settlement built (lines 54-62.1)

Rukiza's sister, not previously mentioned, is a beautiful woman and becomes known to King Ruhinda (lines 63-66.4). The king sends matchmakers to arrange a marriage, Rukiza specifies the brideprice payment, and the king provides it (lines 67-74.1). Then Rukiza specifies the amount of beer required for the prenuptial fete (lines 75-78.2).
About a year later, men accuse Rukiza to the king of flaunting the social value of cattle; the king is outraged and orders Rukiza's cattle to be confiscated (lines 80-91).

One man leaves the court and travels to Rukiza's settlement, meeting herdsmen milking cattle; despite his poor appearance, the man claims to have established a blood brotherhood with Rukiza (lines 92-98.1).

Notified by his herdsmen, Rukiza has the man properly clothed and grants him an audience (lines 99-103). The man recounts the accusation to Rukiza and warns him about the impending attack (lines 104-106.1).
Rukiza summons his clansmen-warriors and deploys them for battle (lines 107-16).
When the king's warriors come to confiscate Rukiza's cattle, the clansmen drive them off, killing many in the process (lines 117-24).

Rukiza spares the life of the single remaining royal warrior; he mutilates him and sends him to the king with a defiant message (lines 125-27). The king again summons warriors and attacks Rukiza, with similar results (lines 128-33).
King Ruhinda accepts the advice of his counselors to stop the attacks (lines 134-38).

Another year passed, and Rukiza's sister began bearing children, three in number, including the designated royal heir (lines 138.1-140). After several more years, the king asks Rukiza's sister for the secret of her brother's strength; at first she demurs, but after warning that she would exact swift revenge for Rukiza's death, she is convinced no harm will come to him (lines 141-147.2).

She tells of his magical multiple hearts, which are located outside Rukiza's body (lines 148-53.1). First confirming the information through divination, the king summons his warriors (lines 155-59.1).

The king orders archers to pierce the magical hearts; they do so (lines 160-63.1). The archers call Rukiza out to battle; in his weakened state he is soon slain and his cattle driven off (lines 164-74).

Seeing Rukiza's cattle mixed with the royal herd, Rukiza's sister learns of her brother's death (lines 175-81). Using a ruse, she lures the king into a vulnerable position and cuts his throat (lines 181.1-86.2).
She returns to Rukiza's settlement and buries him; Rukiza's clan forever lacks land to settle on (lines 187-202.2).
